Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
v
Preface
The Physical Education and Sport Teaching Module for Form 1 has been developed in 
response to the introduction of the Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC), a transformative 
initiative aimed at enhancing quality education and relevance.
This teaching module is designed to support teachers to effectively deliver Physical Education 
and Sport lessons under the new curriculum. Its primary objectives are to, bridge the gap created 
by the absence of approved textbooks and equip teachers with practical skills and assessments 
tailored to learners’ needs and levels.
The Ministry of Education acknowledges the challenges associated with transitioning to a new 
curriculum. Consequently, this module reflects our commitment to providing teachers with 
structured guidance, suggested activities, formative and summative assessments aligned with 
the CBC’s objectives.
While this module serves as a foundational resource, we encourage teachers to supplement it 
with their own research and innovations to address potential gaps during implementation. The 
suggested activities and assessments are designed to foster practical and critical thinking skills 
in learners, ensuring relevance and adaptability across diverse school contexts.
Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom dynamics and 
explore alternative approaches when necessary. We hope this module will empower teachers 
to navigate the new competence-based curriculum with confidence, inspiring and equipping 
learners with applied knowledge, skills, and values.
Kelvin Mambwe  (PhD)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Physical Education and Sport

Their invaluable input and expertise have enriched the content and quality of this teaching

I also acknowledge the dedication and hard work of the staff at the Curriculum Development

development process.
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director - Curriculum Development
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Terminology

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
viii
INTRODUCTION 
The Competence-Based Curriculum (CBC) is designed to equip learners with competences 
necessary to navigate the complexities of the modern world. This Physical Education and Sport 
(PES) teaching module is tailored to support teachers in implementing the CBC, focusing on 
the attainment of specific competences of specified sub-topics as indicated in the syllabus, but 
aligned to the general competences like creativity and innovation, critical thinking, problem-
solving, and effective communication.
Each specific competence is supported by carefully designed learning activities that cater for 
diverse learning styles and abilities. These learning activities further give guidance on how they 
should be effectively administered to the learners to meet the expected standards that speak to the 
specific competences. Furthermore, this module encourages teachers to assess learner progress 
using a range of strategies.
By using this PES teaching module, teachers will be able to support learners to acquire competences 
outlined in the syllabus. In turn, learners will develop practical skills and enhancement of talents, 
creativity, self-expression and a sense of aesthetics.

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
1
1.9 SPORTS SKILLS DEVELOPMENT
INTRODUCTION
Sports skill development refers to the process of acquiring, improving and refining the physical, 
technical, mental and social abilities required to participate and excel in various sports. This 
includes physical attributes such as speed, agility, endurance, strength, balance, and coordination.
In addition to physical training, effective sports development also focuses on mental skills like 
focus, decision-making, and resilience, as well as social skills such as teamwork, leadership, 
discipline, and communication.
This topic equips learners with a comprehensive understanding of the techniques, strategies, and 
demands of different sports, categorised into athletics, ball games, board games, and racquet 
games.
Learners should acquire the following general competencies:
Communication: Ability to effectively convey and receive information.
Citizenship: Accept responsibility for one’s deeds through sportsmanship.
Collaboration: Appreciate and acknowledge the views of group members while participating in 
activities.
Problem-Solving: Capacity to adapt to new situations and challenges.
Emotional Intelligence: Express self-awareness, self-management and build relationships with 
others.
Analytical thinking: Ability to observe, navigate, analyze, design, judge and depict objects either 
natural or artificial.
Creativity and Innovation: Apply knowledge and skills in new and creative ways. 
TERMINOLOGY
     
Adaptability: Adjusting to changing circumstances and opponents.
Algebraic notation: This is a standard system used to record and describe moves in 
chess.
Castling: A special move in chess that allows a player to move the king and a rook at 
the same time. 
Crosscourt: A shot hit diagonally into the opposite court
Distance Running: Running over longer distances, requiring endurance and stamina.
Double hit: An illegal hit in which the racket contacts the shuttle twice in one swing
Dribbling: Controlling a ball with the hands or feet while moving.
En passant: a special pawn capture in chess that occurs under a specific condition.
Face: The string area of the racket
Footwork: Moving efficiently and effectively around the court.
Pattern Recognition: Identifying and exploiting patterns in the game
Shooting: Propelling the ball toward a goal or target.	
Sprinting: Running at maximum speed over a short distance.
Stroke Production: Generating power and control when hitting the ball.
Torso: the central part of the human body which refers to the trunk

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
2
Sub-Topic 1.9.1 Athletics
Introduction:
Athletics is a core sport that comprises various track (running) and field (jumping and throwing) 
events. It is one of the oldest and most popular sports worldwide, focusing on individual 
performance and physical fitness. 
In this topic, learners will focus on sprints which are short distance races that test speed and 
explosive power, middle distance races which focus on both speed and endurance as well as 
relay races which focuses on teamwork, timing and coordination. Through performing these 
skills, learners will improve their running techniques, physical conditioning and team work, 
while gaining confidence and self-esteem in their athletic abilities. 
Specific Competence(s) 1.9.1.1 Perform skills in track events
TIP
    
The lesson on skills in track events can be conducted in a Natural environment (the running 
track). The teacher can also teach the learners from any of the environments such as Artificial 
(Modified classroom…) and Technological (video clips, shows...).
Teaching/learning material
Running spikes or athletic shoes, starting blocks (for sprints), relay batons, whistle, stop watch, 
track markings, cones, measuring tapes… (locally improvised materials; flat rubbers or plastic 
sandals, wooden blocks or bricks as starting blocks, road up paper or sticks as relay batons, chalk 
or strings for marking lanes on the ground, home-made stop watch using mobile phones or timer 
apps…)
 Learning Activity
 Performing skills in track events. (Sprints, middle-distance, relays…).
DISCUSS
Learners should: 
Task 1 
Discuss and mark the running track using the standard dimensions.
CONTENT TIP
    
Track Events

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
3
A standard running track is 400m long, and has eight (8) lanes which are staggered at the starting 
points.
ACTIVITY
Learners should: 
Task 2 
In smaller groups, perform skills in the following categories in track events. (Sprints, 
middle-distance, relays…).
 Sprints
These are short races that are run at maximum speed with explosive strength, full force 
and high intensity. The following are the categories of sprints (dashes).
 60 m 
 100 m
 200 m
 400 m

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
4
 4 x 100 m relay
 4 x 400 m relay
 Medley relay
Basic rules for sprints
The following are the basic rules that should be adhered to:
 Running on lanes
 Obeying the commands- “On your marks, Set, Go (gun firing, whistling, clapping, 
shouting…)
 No athlete should cross the starting lane before gun-firing or whistling.
 When using starting blocks, some foot part should be in contact with the track
 Should an individual/ athlete continue false starting twice then he/ she is disqualified
 When a false start is made, all athletes are on a yellow card & whoever makes a 
false start again receives a red card and automatically sent off/ disqualified.
 If the runner falls down on reaching the finishing line and his hands & feet are 
inside the track his race is not considered to be complete.
Relay races. 
 Relays are team events featuring four runners who should run the same distance. 
They run with a baton which must be passed on from one runner to another.  
Types of relay races
 4x100m
 4x400m
 Medley 
Rules governing relay races
 Stations for teams are drawn for each team retains/ keeps each station throughout 
the race. This mostly apply in 4x100m relay 
 In the 4x400m, the first lap will be run in lanes. Part of the second lap, up to the 
end of the first curve (100m) will also be run in lanes. After this, the runners then 
are free to take any position on the track. Staggered starts are used in this relay.
 The baton must be passed inside the 20m passing/ exchange zone
 The baton must be carried in the hand throughout the race
 After passing the baton, the runner may not interfere with an opponent
 A baton dropped within the zone may be collected by either outgoing or incoming 
runner. 
 A baton dropped outside the zone must be collected by the runner who dropped it.
 For relay legs that are 200m or less, the outgoing runner may line up 10m in front 
of the exchange zone, but the pass must occur within the 20m exchange zone.
 Assistance by pushing off/ any other method will cause disqualification of his/her 
team.
 No competitors may run two sections for a team.

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
5
Middle distance race
 These are races longer than sprint/dashes and require a combination of speed, endurance 
and strategy.
Types of Middle distance race
 800 m
 1500 m
Rules governing middle races
 No obstruction of other runners
 One false start results in disqualification
 No starting blocks.
 In 800m race, runners must stay in their assigned lanes until they reach the break 
line. 
 1500m usually start in a curve, with a break line after the first bend.
 A runner may overtake another only when it is safe and without contact.
 Overtaking only happens on the outside and not the inside of the curve.
 The winner is the runner whose torso first crosses the finish line (not arms or head)
Expected standards 
Skills in track events performed properly.
Sub-Topic:1.9.2 Ball games
Introduction: 
Ball games are sporting activities that involve the use of a ball and require players to apply 
various physical skills and tactics to score points or goals. These games promote physical fitness 
while also fostering important values such as teamwork, cooperation, communication, and fair 
play. 
This sub-topic focuses on the two ball games being Football and Netball skills. Both sports 
demand a blend of individual skill, tactical awareness, and team coordination. Major skills such 
as dribbling, passing, shooting, catching, and marking form the foundation of effective play in 
these games. Developing these skills enhances not only personal performance but also overall 
team success.
Specific Competence 1.9.2.1 Perform skills in Football and Netball games.
Learning environment:
The lesson on Football and Netball can be conducted in a Natural environment (football pitch 
and netball court). The teacher can also teach the learners from any of the environments such as 
Artificial (Modified classroom…) and Technological (video clips, shows...).
Teaching/learning material
Official footballs and Netballs, nets, bibs or team jerseys, whistle, goal posts, cones…(locally 
improvised materials; rolled up clothing or plastics as balls, sticks or stuck stones as goal posts, 
empty bottles or stones as markers

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
6
 Learning Activity:
 Performing basic skills in Football and Netball games. (Dribbling, shooting, 
catching, passing, marking…).
Learners should:
DISCUSS
Task 1
Discuss and mark the football pitch using the standard dimensions.
TIP
Dimensions of a standard football pitch:
Field
 The rectangular playing field may measure from 90m to 120m long and 45m to 
90m wide.
 A half way line must be marked out across the field. 
 A suitable mark in the center of the field is surrounded by a circle with a radius of 
9.15m.
Goal area
 Two lines are drawn at right angles to the goal line, 5.50 m from each goal post. 
These lines extend into the field for 5.50 m and are joined by a line drawn parallel 
with the goal-line.

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
7
Penalty area
 Two lines are drawn at right angles to the goal-line, 16.50m from each goal post. 
These lines extend into the field for 16.50 m and are joined by a line drawn parallel 
with the goal line.  
 The penalty spot is drawn 11 m from the centre of the goal-line. Using the penalty 
spot as the centre, an arc of a circle with a radius of 9.15 m is drawn outside the 
penalty area.
Corner area
 A quarter circle with a radius of 1 m is drawn from each corner post. 
Goal post
 Goal posts are 7.32 m apart and are joined by a horizontal crossbar. The lower 
edge of which is 2.44 m from the ground. 
 Nets are attached to the post, crossbar and the ground behind the goals. 
ACTIVITY
Learners should:
Task 2     
In pairs or groups perform various activities to demonstrate skills like dribbling, 
shooting and passing.
Dribbling
TIP
Focus on control, balance, and movement in small spaces.
 Each pair sets up a cone 5–10 meters away. 
 One partner dribbles to the cone and back, then hands over the ball to the other. 
 Focus on using both feet.
Passing
TIP
Emphasise accuracy, timing, and proper technique.
 Partners stand 3–5 meters apart and try passing with only one touch 
 After each pass, the player moves to a new position. 
 This teaches movement off the ball.
Shooting
TIP
 Maintain proper body posture, aiming, and follow-through.
 One partner passes the ball to the other who takes a shot at the goal/net. 
 Switch roles after every shot.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
8
Learners should
DISCUSS
Task 1
Discuss and mark the Netball court using the standard dimensions.                     
NETBALL
The court
 The court is 30.5m long and 15.25m wide. The longer sides (length) are called 
sidelines and the shorter side (width) is the goal-line. 
 The court is divided into three equal parts, the centre third and two goal thirds.
 A semicircle with a radius of 4.9m and with its centre in the middle of the goal is 
drawn in each goal third. This semicircle is called the goal circle. 
 A circle with a diameter of 90cm marks the centre of the court. This is called the 
centre circle.
ACTIVITY
Learners should
Task 2.
In pairs or groups perform passing, shooting … skills in Netball 
Passing
TIP

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
9
 Accuracy and Teamwork
 Split into groups of 3–5 players.
 Set cones in a line. Players pass the ball down the line using chest passes, shoulder 
passes, or bounce passes.
 After passing, players move to the back of the line.
 Shooting 
 
TIPS
Aiming and Technique
 In small groups, rotate through different shooting spots around the goalpost.
 Each player takes a shot from each cone or mark and collects their ball before 
rotating.
 Use one hand to guide and the other to support the ball.
 Bend knees slightly and extend arms upwards when releasing.
 Aim just above the ring for a smooth arc.   
Expected standards 
Skills in Football and Netball games performed correctly.
Sub-Topic: 1.9.3 Board games.
Introduction: 
Board games are mentally engaging activities played on a flat surface, requiring strategic thinking, 
concentration, and careful planning. Unlike physical games, they focus on cognitive skills such 
as reasoning, memory and patience. 
This sub-topic focuses on the basic skills in Chess and Draughts.  These two are known for their 
ability to sharpen the mind and promote decision-making. Both are widely played in schools and 
competitions, encouraging intellectual growth. These offer fun, challenge, and valuable mental 
training that benefit learners in and beyond the classroom.
Specific Competence:  1.9.3.1 Perform skills in Chess and Draught.    
The lessons on the basic skills of board games can be conducted in a Natural (indoor and out­
door…). and Technological (video clips, computer…) environments
Teaching /learning materials
Standard Chess and draught boards with pieces, chess notation booklets or score sheets, digital 
chess boards or apps for demonstration. (Locally improvised materials; drawn chess/draught 
boards on paper, cardboard or wood, bottle caps, curved wood pieces, handwritten sheets for 
notation practices, chalkboard or white board to illustrate moves and strategies.)
Learning Activities:
 Performing basic skills in Chess and Draught. (Castling, en passant, basic algebraic 
notation…).

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
10
Chess 
DISCUSS
Learners should:
Task 1
Analyse the chess board and its pieces:
TIPS
 Chess is a strategic board game played between two players on an 8×8 checkered 
board. Each player begins with 16 pieces: one king, one queen, two rooks, two 
bishops, two knights, and eight pawns. 
 The aim is to checkmate your opponent’s king, putting it in a position where it is 
under threat and cannot escape capture.
Basic Rules of Play
 The white side always moves first.
 Players take turns, moving one piece at a time.
 Each piece moves in a specific way:
How Chess Pieces Move
King (♔ / ♚):
 Moves one square in any direction: forward, backward, sideways, or diagonally.
 The king is the most important piece-if it is checkmated, the game ends.
Queen (♕ / ♛):
 Moves in any direction (forward, backward, sideways, or diagonally) for any 
number of squares.
 The queen is the most powerful piece.

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
11
Rook/ Castle (♖ / ♜):
 Moves in a straight line: horizontally or vertically for any number of squares.
 Two rooks start in each corner of the board.
Bishop (♗ / ♝):
 Moves diagonally for any number of squares.
 Each bishop stays on the same color square for the entire game.
Knight (♘ / ♞):
 Moves in an L-shape: two squares in one direction, then one square at a right 
angle (or vice versa).
 Knights are the only pieces that can jump over others.
Pawn (♙ / ♟):
 Moves forward one square, but captures diagonally.
 On its first move, a pawn can move forward two squares.
TIP
Special rules for pawns:
 Promotion: When a pawn reaches the far end of the board, it can be promoted to a 
queen, rook, bishop, or knight—most often to a queen.
 En passant: A special pawn capture that can happen when an opponent moves a 
pawn two squares forward from its starting position, and it lands next to your pawn. 
You may capture it as if it had moved only one square.
 Blocking: Pawns cannot move forward if a piece is directly in front of them
🏰 Special Move – Castling
 Castling is a move involving the king and one of the rooks.
 It allows the king to move two squares toward a rook, and the rook jumps over the 
king to the next square.
TIP
This can only be done if;
 Neither the king nor rook has moved.
 The squares between them are empty
 The king is not in check, nor passes through or ends on a square under attack.
✍️
 Basic Algebraic Notation
 Algebraic notation is the standard system used to record and describe moves.
 Each square on the board has a name (e4, d5…) based on file (a–h) and rank (1–8).

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
12
TIP
Pieces are abbreviated as:
 K = King, Q = Queen, R = Rook, B = Bishop, N = Knight, (no letter for pawns).
(Nf3 means “Knight to f3”, e4 means “pawn to e4”…)
ACTIVITY
Learners should
Task 2.
In pairs perform various basic skills in Chess (castling, en passant, basic algebraic 
notation …).as guided by the teacher.
TIP
The teacher should look for the following as the learners play the game;
Careful thinking.
 Create mini-scenarios where castling or en passant is possible and have them 
identify and execute the move.
Planning ahead.
 Show simple piece movement in notation, then special moves: (kingside castling), 
(queenside castling)
 Using each piece wisely. 
 Developing strategies to control the board.
 Checkmate the opponent’s king.
Draughts/checkers
DISCUSS
Learners should:
Task 1
Discuss, make and familiarises themselves with the draughts Board 
The draughts Board

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
13
Be introduced to Draughts and how it is played.
CONTENT TIPS
 
 Draughts, also called Checkers, is a two-player board game that combines strategy, 
planning, and tactical movement.
 
 It is played on a checkered board, typically 8x8 (standard version), using 12 pieces 
per player, placed on the dark squares of the first three rows.
 
 The game is easy to learn but challenging to master. 
 
 Players move their pieces diagonally across the board with the aim to capture all the 
opponent’s pieces or block them from moving.
How Draughts Pieces Move
Regular Pieces:
 Move diagonally forward by one square.
 Can capture an opponent’s piece by jumping over it diagonally onto an empty 
square.
 Multiple captures are allowed and must be taken in one turn if available.
 Compulsory capturing: if a player can make a capture, they must do so.
Kings
 A piece becomes a king when it reaches the opposite end of the board.
 Kings can move and capture diagonally in both directions-forward and backward.
 Kings follow the same jumping rules as regular pieces but with more freedom of 
movement.
Basic Rules of Draughts
Starting the Game:
 Each player places 12 pieces on the dark squares of the three rows closest to them.
 One player uses dark-colored pieces; the other uses light-colored pieces.
 The player with the dark pieces moves first.
Movement:
 Only dark squares are used.
 Regular pieces move forward diagonally.
 Kings can move forward and backward diagonally.
Capturing:
 A player must capture if possible.
 Multiple jumps are allowed and must be played in a single turn.
 Captures can be made with both regular pieces and kings.
Winning the Game:
A player wins by;
 Capturing all opponent’s pieces.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
14
 Blocking all of the opponent’s pieces so they have no legal move.
Draw (Tie):
 A game may end in a draw if neither player can force a win (stalemate or agreement).
ACTIVITY
Learners should:
Task 2
In pairs perform various basic skills in draught. 
TIPS
  
The teacher should look for the following when learners are performing;
 How the pieces move, including capturing and kinging.
 Basic strategies, such as controlling the center, building a prime and creating an­
chors
Sub-Topic: 1.9.4 Racquet games
Introduction: 
Badminton is a dynamic and fast-paced racquet sport played either by two opponents (singles) or 
two pairs (doubles). The game takes place on a rectangular court divided by a net, where players 
use lightweight racquet to hit a shuttlecock back and forth. The main objective is to score points 
by landing the shuttlecock in the opponent’s court or causing them to commit a fault.
In addition to being a competitive sport, badminton is a fun and engaging physical activity that 
improves coordination, agility, reflexes and overall fitness. Whether played for recreation or 
competition, learning the basic skills is essential for building confidence and competence on the 
court.
 This sub-topic introduces learners to the fundamentals of racquet games like badminton. Learners 
will learn the basic skills, tactics and strategies required to play each game, as well as the rules 
and etiquette involved.
Specific Competence(s) 1.9.4.1 Perform skills in Badminton.
TIPS
The lesson on the skills in badminton can be taught in a natural environment (indoor court…), It 
can also be conducted from any of the environments such as Artificial (Modified Gymnasium…) 
and Technological (video clips…)
Teaching/learning materials
Racquets, shuttlecock, whistle, net, poles (locally improvised materials; hand-made racquets 
made of wire, wood, plastic frames with string, shuttlecock made of chicken feathers.

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
15
Learning Activity:
 Performing basic skills in Badminton. (Grip of the racquet, strokes, footwork…)
ACTIVITY
Learners should:
Task 1
 Mark the Badminton court using standard dimensions and discuss the playing 
equipment. 
CONTENT TIPS
      
The Badminton Court
The Net
 The net must be firmly stretched from post to post and must be 76 cm deep. The 
top of the net must be 1.524 m above the floor at centre and 1.55 m at the goal post.
  It must be edged with a white tape that is 7.5 cm wide and doubled and it must be 
supported by a cord or cable run through the tape and strained over and flush with 
the top of the posts.
Posts
 The posts, 1.55 m high, should be sufficiently rigid to support the net under tension. 
They are placed on the side boundary lines of the court.
TIPS
Playing equipment
Shuttlecock

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
16
 The shuttle is generally made of 16 feathers fixed to a cork base. 
 The weight varies between 4.74 g and 5.50g. 
Racquet 
 The racquet is usually made of graphite. 
 The frame of the racquet, including the handle, may not exceed 68 cm in overall 
length and 23 cm in overall width.
  The stringed area may not exceed 28 cm in overall length and 22 cm in overall 
width.
How the game is played
 The doubles game is played by two players to a side, and the singles game by one 
player to a side.
Scoring
 A match shall consist of best of three games, unless otherwise arranged.
 The side which first scores 21 points shall win a game.
 The side winning a rally shall add a point to its score.
 If the score become 20-all, the side which scores two consecutive points shall win 
the game.
 If the score become 29-all, the side scoring 30th point shall win the game.
 The side winning a game serves first in the next game.
Learners should
Task 2
Perform the basic skills in Badminton. (grip of the racquet, strokes, footwork…)

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
17
CONTENT TIPS
    
The grips
When the learners are playing the game, the teacher should assess the correctness of the 
Forehand and Backhand grip
 Hold the shaft of the racquet with your non preferred hand.
 Make sure the face of the racquet is perpendicular to the floor.
 Shake hands with the handle of the racquet.
 Make a “V” formed by the juncture of your thumb and index finger.
 Grasp the handle lightly, with fingers spread slightly.
 When not in the act of stroking the shuttle, the grip should be relaxed and fairly 
loose.
 Tighten the grip enough to maintain control of the racquet.

Skills in badminton performed correctly.

 Athletics, ball games, and racket games comprise a diverse range of sports that 
cater to various interests and skill levels.
 
 Athletics encompasses track and field events, road running, and cross-country 
running, demanding speed, strength, endurance, and agility. 
 
 Ball games, including football and netball require hand-eye coordination, speed, 
and strategy, while racket games like badminton involve hitting a shuttlecock with 
a racket, necessitating quick reflexes and precise technique.
 
 Collectively, these sports offer a platform for physical exercise, social interaction 
and competitive excellence.

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
19
TOPIC 1.10 ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY
INTRODUCTION
Anatomy and Physiology is the study of the human body’s structure and function.  In Physical 
Education and Sport, it helps learners understand how the body moves, responds to exercise and 
how it adapts to physical activity.
This topic aims to introduce learners to the fundamental principles of anatomy and physiology, 
focusing on their application to physical education and sports. Learners will explore the human 
body structure and function, laying the groundwork for further study in exercise science and 
sports performance.
General Competences:
Learners should acquire the following general competences:
Problem solving:  The application of knowledge to tackle real life situations using various strat­
egies and providing solutions to challenging situations within the environment.
Critical thinking: The ability to breakdown complex ideas and skills to improve the ability to 
understand.
Analytical thinking: The capacity to observe, navigate, analyse, design, judge and represent 
objects whether natural or artificial.
Communication: use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations.
TERMINOLOGY
Appendicular: The part of the skeletal system that includes the upper and the lower 
limb as well as the bones that connect them to the axial skeleton.
Axial: The part of the skeletal system that forms the central axis of the body
 Bone: Hard, calcified tissue
Cartilage: Connective tissue that provide that provides cushioning, support and smooth 
movement between bones and joints.
Joint: A place where two or more bones join together.
Ligament: Fibrous connective tissue that connects bones or connects bone to bone.
Skeleton: internal framework of the body, composed of all the bones and cartilages 
that provide support, protection and movement.
Tendon: Fibrous connective tissue that connects muscles to bones.
Sub-Topic: 1.10.1 Human Skeletal System
Introduction
The Skeletal System is a vital part of the human body, providing support, protection and 
movement. It serves as a foundation of the body’s structure and movement, enabling human 
beings to stand, walk, run and engage in various physical activities. 
In this sub-topic, learners will explore the structure and function of the skeletal system, including

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
20
bones, joints and ligaments. 
Specific Competence(s): 1.10.1.1 Analyse the human skeletal system
Learning Environment:
The teacher can also teach the lesson from any of the environment such as Natural (biology) 
laboratory …), Artificial (modified classroom…) and Technological (video clips, shows…)
Teaching/learning material
Charts/posters showing labelled human skeleton, the skeletal model, anatomy text books, projec­
tor, white board (locally improvised materials; skeleton made from cardboard, sticks or straws to 
show body parts, drawn skeleton on manila paper…)
Learners’ activity
 Identifying the framework of the human skeletal system and its functions. (Axial and 
appendicular skeleton).
Learners should:
DISCUSS
Task 1 
Work in smaller groups to identify and label the framework of the human skeletal 
system (Axial and Appendicular. 
TIPS

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
21
Human skeleton:
 The skeleton has 206 bones, held together by strong fibers called ligaments. 
Axial Skeleton:
 Skull (Cranium and Face Bones)
 Vertebral Column (Cervical, Thoracic, Lumbar, Sacrum, Coccyx)
 Ribcage (Ribs and Sternum)
Appendicular Skeleton:
 Pectoral Girdle (Scapula and Clavicle)
 Upper Limbs (Humerus, Radius, Ulna, Carpals, Metacarpals, Phalanges)
 Pelvic Girdle (lllium, Ischium, Pubis)
 Lower Limbs (Femur, Patella, Tibia, Fibula, Tarsals, Metatarsals, Phalanges)
DISCUSS
Learners should
Task 2
Work in smaller groups to discuss the functions of the human skeletal system.
TIP
 support
 The human skeleton forms a framework to support the body and give it shape.
 protection 
 The human skeleton provides protection to the internal organs, such as the brain 
(Skull), heart and lungs (ribcage), and spinal cord (vertebral column).
movement 
 The human skeleton works with muscles to facilitate moving allowing for 
locomotion, manipulation and other physical activities.
Blood cells production.
 The bones in the human skeletal system, particularly the pelvis, vertebrae, and ribs, 
are responsible for producing blood cells. (white, red and platelets)
Storage for essential minerals
 Bones act as a storage of calcium and phosphorus, which are vital for various body 
functions.
 Bones produce hormones like osteocalcin, which helps regulate calcium levels and 
energy metabolism.
Learning activity: Identifying the types of joints (freely moveable, slight moveable,

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
22
immovable joints).
DISCUSS
 
Learners should
Task 3
Work in groups to identify and examine the types of joints.
TIPS
A joint is a place or position where two or more bones meet. They are divided into three 
types namely freely moveable (Synovial joint), slight moveable (cartilaginous joint) and 
immovable (fibrous joint).
Synovial/ freely movable joints
 
 At a freely moveable joint, the bones can move quite freely. 
 These are the majority of joints which allow the greatest amount of movement 
(knee joint, elbow joint, shoulder join, hip joint, finger joint…)
 At a freely moveable joint, the bones can move quite freely. 
 These are the majority of joints which allow the greatest amount of movement 
(knee joint, elbow joint, shoulder join, hip joint, finger joint…)
Parts of a synovial/ freely movable joint
The joint capsule:
 It holds the bones together and protects the joint. It is an extension of the skin/ Peri­
osteum that covers each bone.
Synovial membrane

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
23
 Lines the capsule and oozes a slippery liquid called Synovial fluid.
A Joint cavity: 
 This is a small gape between the bones. It is filled with Synovial fluid. This lubri­
cates the joint so that the bones can move more easily.
Cartilage
 This is a connective tissue which acts as a buffer/ shock absorber between the bones 
and joints.
Ligaments
 This is a connective tissue that joins bones to bones.
Tendon
 This is a strong, fibrous connective tissue that attaches muscles to bones
TIPS
 
The teacher to emphasise on other types of the joints and their positions in rela­
tion to synovial joints

Flexion
 Flexion means bending part of the body. Flexion is nearly always forward. 
Extension
 Extension means straightening part of the body to its normal position.
Rotation
 This is a circular movement. Rotation is turning movement around an imaginary 
line, like a wheel turning on its axis e.g. turning your head.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
24
Circumduction
 This is also a circular movement. In Circumduction, the end of a bone moves in a 
circle e.g. swinging your arm in a circle when bowling.
Abduction
 Abduction is a sideways movement of a limb, out from the centre line.
Adduction
 Adduction is a sideways movement of a limb, towards and even across the centre 
line. (Adduction is towards the middle)
Slightly movable cartilaginous joints
 The bones at a slightly movable joint can move only a little.
 They are held together by strong white cords or straps called ligaments and joined 
by cartilage.
 This is like a fibrous cushion which stops the bones from knocking together. It can 
squash a little to let them move. 
 The joints between most of your vertebrae are slightly moveable. The pads of car­
tilage between them act as shock absorbers to prevent friction.
 The joints between your ribs and sternum are also slightly moveable. They move a 
little when you breathe in and out.
Immovable/ Fibrous/ fixed joints

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
25
 The bones at a fixed joint cannot move at all. 
 They interlock/ overlap, and are held close together by strong by tough fiber e.g. 
skull bone, & the pelvis

SUMMARY
 
 Application of the principles of anatomy and physiology helps athletes and coaches 
to optimize performance, prevent injuries and promote overall health and well-
being as well as enhancing recovery and rehabilitation.
 
 The skeletal system provides the framework for the human body, allowing for 
movement, support and protection.
 
 The axial and appendicular skeletal work together to provide a wide range of 
movement and functions. Axial malfunction can lead to serious health problems.
 
 The bones of the human body are connected by joints which play different roles in 
enhancing movements. Their functions are for movement, stability, weight bearing 
and shock absorption.

TOPIC: 1.11  SPORTS BIOMECHANICS
Introduction
Sports Biomechanics is the study of how the human body moves during sports and exercise. 
It uses ideas from science and physics to help athletes and coaches/sports teachers understand 
movements like running, jumping, kicking and throwing. 
This knowledge helps improve performance, develop better techniques and prevent injuries. For 
example, biomechanics can help a footballer kick more accurately or a sprinter take off faster. It 
also plays an important role in designing sports equipment, such as shoes, balls and uniforms, to 
match how the body moves. This topic will help learners/athletes acquire proper movements that 
can help them stay safe, perform better and enjoy an active and healthy lifestyle.
Learners should acquire the following general competencies:
Analytical Thinking: The capability to observe, navigate, analyse, design, judge, and represent 
objects, whether natural or artificial.
Critical Thinking: The ability to breakdown complex ideas and skills to improve the ability to 
understand.
Problem Solving: The application of knowledge to tackle real-life issues using various strategies 
and providing solutions to challenging situations within one’s environment.
TERMINOLOGY
Acceleration: Rate of change of velocity.
Centre of mass: A point in an object or system where all of its mass can be considered 
to be concentrated.
Distance: Total length of the path covered by an object as it moves from one point to 
another.
Force: Push or Pull that causes an object to change its motion
Friction: Refers to the force that opposes motion between two surfaces that are in 
contact with each other.
Gravity: The force that pulls objects towards the center of the Earth.
Kinetic friction: The force that opposes motion when an object is already moving.
Mass: Amount of matter contained in an object.
Motion: Change in position of an object over time.
Net force: Also known as the resultant force, a net force is the final force acting on a 
body once all the other forces acting have been considered.
Scalar quantity: As magnitude only.
Speed: Rate of change of the position of a body
Static friction: The force that prevents an object from moving when it is stationary.
Vector quantity: As magnitude and direction.
Weight: Measure of force exerted on an object by gravity.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
28
Sub-Topic: 1.11.1- Force
Introduction:
Force is a push or pull that can cause an object to start moving, stop, change direction, or alter its 
speed. It is a fundamental concept that plays a vital role in understanding motion.
This sub-topic explores how force affects linear motion, which is the motion of an object moving 
in a straight line. While the laws of physics suggest that an object or athlete in motion should 
continue moving indefinitely in a straight line at a constant speed, this rarely happens in sports. 
This is because various forces such as gravity, friction, and air resistance are always acting on 
the moving body. These forces work together to slow down, alter or completely stop motion. 
By studying force, learners can better understand why athletes and objects don’t stay in motion 
forever and how forces shape movement in sporting activities.
Specific Competence:  1.11.1.1 Illustrate forces in sporting activities.
The lesson on force in sporting activities can be conducted from any of the environment such 
as Natural (playing grounds…), Artificial (Modified classroom…) and Technology (video clips, 
show…)
Teaching/ learning materials
Balls, surfaces with different textures, Spring balance, small weights, calculators, stop watch, 
measuring tape/meter ruler, cones or markers, whistle, (Locally improvised materials; grass, 
sand, smooth floor, plastic bottles, carpet)…
Learning Activity:
 Demonstrating forces in sporting activities. (Friction, gravity, weight…).
Learners should:
DISCUSS
Task 1
Work in smaller groups to demonstrate different forces in sporting activities.
Friction
TIPS
Observe how friction affects movement of the ball on smooth and rough surfaces 
(track, grass, concrete, …)
 Roll the same ball across each surface.
 Measure how far the ball travels on each.
 Learners record and compare results.
 Discuss which surface had the most friction and how this relates to sports surfaces

Teaching Module
29

 Gravity
TIPS
Appreciate the force of gravity by dropping two balls of different sizes.
 Drop both balls from the same height.
 Observe that they hit the ground at the same time (gravity acts equally).
 Discuss how gravity affects falling in sports
Task 3
 Weight 
TIP
Observe how weight affects forces in sporting activities
 Use different ball as weights for lifting or throwing.
 Compare different weights to understand how heavier objects affect force in sports 
(shot put or weight lifting)
 Discuss how weight affects force.
TIP
Teacher to guide learners to perform different activities related to the above types of 
forces. 
Learning activity
 Calculating simple forces in relation to sporting activities. (F=ma /or mg).
ACTIVITY

Task 1
Apply the formula F = ma or W = mg. 
 Measure the weight (W = mg) of different sports objects (football, basketball…).
 Calculate the force needed to move them with a given acceleration.
 Discuss how mass and force relate to performance in lifting or throwing?

The teacher can give different examples on calculations of force

30
If a player kicks a 0.45 kg ball and it accelerates at 20 m/s², the force applied to the ball is:
F = ma
= 0.45 kg×20 m/s2
= 9 N
Expected standard:
Forces in sporting activities illustrated correctly
Sub-Topic: 1.11.2 - Speed
Introduction
Speed means how fast someone or something moves. In sports, speed helps athletes do things 
quickly, like running, swimming, or kicking a ball. The faster you move, the better your chance 
of winning in many games.
In physical education, we study speed using Biomechanics. Biomechanics looks at how the body 
moves. It helps us understand how muscles, bones and joints work together to make us move 
faster. For example, a runner uses strong leg muscles and good arm movement to run fast.
This sub-topic explores speed as shown in sports by watching how fast players run, how quickly 
a ball moves or how fast someone swims. In class, speed can be demonstrated by running short 
races or rolling a ball and timing it.                                      
Specific Competence 1.11.2.1 - Illustrate speed in Sporting Activities.
TIPS
The lesson on speed in sporting activities can be taught from any of the environment such as 
Natural (playing grounds…), Artificial (Modified classroom…) and Technology (video clips, 
show…)

 Demonstrating Speed in sporting activities. (Moving objects…).

TOPIC 1.12 ENTREPRENEURSHIP IN SPORTS
Introduction:
Entrepreneurship in sports is about using business ideas to create value in the world of sports. It 
brings together the love for sports and the skills to start and grow businesses. Sports entrepreneurs 
can be involved in many areas, such as organizing sports events, selling sportswear, running 
gyms, or creating new sports apps.
It plays important roles in the sports industry by providing services, creating jobs and helping 
athletes and fans enjoy sports more. Successful sports entrepreneurs also use financial skills to 
manage money well and build strong business portfolios.
The sports industry has many sectors where entrepreneurs can work. These include professional 
teams, sports media, sports equipment, nutrition, fitness and even sports technology. By 
understanding these sectors and learning business skills, learners can discover exciting 
opportunities to become future sports entrepreneurs.
Learners should acquire the following general competencies:
Creativity and Innovation: Apply knowledge and skills in new and creative ways. 
Express one’s idea in a logical manner. Show curiosity about the learning area. Ability to perform 
skills aesthetically.
Financial Literacy: Set and implement financial goals. Understand basic concepts of sources 
of income, saving, budgeting, profit and loss. State the benefits of investing. Evaluate different 
investment opportunities.
Communication: Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations.
Express oneself using different symbols and media. Express oneself (spoken and written) clearly 
and effectively. Give and ask for positive feedback. Appreciate constructive criticism.
Problem-Solving: Use knowledge gained in solving ones problems in real life situations using 
different strategies. Provide solutions to challenging situations within their environment. Use 
pictorial skills to demonstrate basic and complex activities.
TERMINOLOGY
Branding: Creating and promoting a unique identity for a business or product.
Business Model: A plan for generating revenue and sustaining a business
Event management: Planning and executing sports events, such as tournaments or 
games.
Innovation: Introducing new or improved products, services, or processes to the sports 
industry.
Investment: Funding providing to support a business or project.
Marketing Strategy: A plan for promoting a business or product.
Revenue Streams: Sources of income such as sponsorship, ticket sales or merchan­
dise.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
34
Sponsorship: A partnership between a business and a sports team or event, often in­
volving financial support.
Sports Entrepreneurship: The application of entrepreneurial principles and practices 
to the sports industry.
Sports media: Using social media platforms to promote a business or product.
Sports Technology: The use of technology to improve sports.
Target Market: A specific group of consumers targeted by a business or product.
Sub-Topic: 1.12.1- Sports Entrepreneurship
Introduction:
Sports Entrepreneurship involves creating and managing businesses that cater various aspects 
of the sports industry. This includes ventures such as Sports Academies, Goods Production, 
Sports Media, Event Management, Sports Technology, and Fitness Centers. Entrepreneurs in 
sports bring innovation and interest to the sports industry, identifying gaps and developing new 
products or services that enhance the sports experience for athletes, fans and stakeholders.
Specific Competence: - 1.12.1.1 Analyse entrepreneurship and its roles in the sports industry.
The lesson on entrepreneurship in sports can be taught from any of the environment such as Nat­
ural (classroom…), Artificial (computer labs…) and Technology (video clips, show…)
Teaching /learning materials
Computers, video clips, calculators, charts, graphs, posters, Markers (Locally Materials Impro­
vised Old sports shoes, jerseys, or equipment, hand-drawn diagrams or posters made by learn­
ers skipping ropes, weights with bottles…)…
Learning Activity:
Discussing sports entrepreneurship.
Learners should
DISCUSS
Task 1
Work in groups to discuss sports entrepreneurship.

and who benefits.
 Sports academy
 Fitness center
 Sports media

Teaching Module
35
 Event management
 Sports technology
 Sports equipment
TIP

 To train and develop athletes in specific sports through structured programs and

 Young athletes, schools, professional clubs, communities

Learning Activity:
Analysing the roles of entrepreneurship in the sports industry. (Financial literacy, busi­
ness portfolio…).
DISCUSS
Learners should 
Debate on the roles of entrepreneurship in sports industry.
TIP
Task 1
Debate motions
 Financial literacy is the most important skill for a sports entrepreneur.
 Sports entrepreneurs should focus on building a diverse business portfolio for 
long-term success.

Physical Education and Sport
Teaching Module
36
 Sports businesses should prioritize profitability over social impact.
 Financial planning and budgeting are more important than business innovation in 
sports entrepreneurship.
 Sponsorship and external funding are more important than financial literacy for 
sports entrepreneurs.
TIP
Financial literacy is the most important skill for a sports entrepreneur.
Affirmative Side (Supporting the motion):
 Financial literacy helps sports entrepreneurs make informed decisions about 
budgeting, investments, and managing cash flow.
 It ensures that sports businesses are sustainable and can weather economic 
downturns.
 Without financial understanding, a sports entrepreneur may struggle to make 
strategic decisions or maximize profits, which can lead to business failure.
 Example: A sports academy without proper financial management might fail to 
maintain training equipment or pay its staff on time.
Negative Side (Opposing the motion):
 While financial literacy is important, entrepreneurship also requires creativity, 
leadership, and market understanding.
 Successful entrepreneurs must have a vision for their business, which requires 
strategic planning, innovation, and the ability to adapt to changes in the sports 
industry.
 Example: A sports media startup may thrive due to its innovative content, even 
if the financial management isn’t perfect, as long as they have strong customer 
engagement.
Sports entrepreneurs should focus on building a diverse business portfolio for 
long-term success.
Affirmative Side (Supporting the motion):
 A diverse business portfolio allows sports entrepreneurs to reduce risk by spreading 
investments across multiple revenue streams (e.g., merchandise, event management, 
media rights, and sponsorship).
 Having multiple income sources can protect the business if one sector (e.g., ticket 
sales) declines due to external factors like economic crises or pandemics.
 Example: A sports team owner who invests in both a professional team and a sports 
equipment brand can generate income even if the team faces a poor season.
Negative Side (Opposing the motion):
 Focusing on one niche within the sports industry can lead to more focused expertise, 
which is critical for a business’s success.
 Spreading resources too thin across many sectors can dilute the effectiveness and 
quality of each business venture.
 Example: A sports startup focused only on developing high-quality sports apparel 
might grow more successfully than one trying to do too much, such as managing 
events or running a gym.
Sports businesses should prioritize profitability over social impact.”

Form 1 Term 3
Teaching Module
37
Affirmative Side (Supporting the motion):
 Profitability is essential for the survival and growth of any business, including sports 
businesses. Without strong profit margins, a sports entrepreneur cannot reinvest 
into the business or grow it further.
 In the competitive sports industry, financial sustainability ensures that the business 
can sponsor athletes, create new products, and provide better services.
 Example: A sports equipment company needs to ensure it’s profitable in order to 
reinvest in innovative technology, like better safety equipment for athletes.
Negative Side (Opposing the motion):
 Businesses that focus solely on profitability may neglect their social responsibilities, 
which can harm their reputation and long-term sustainability.
 A social impact focus can build trust with customers, foster brand loyalty, and even 
open up new revenue streams (e.g., social enterprises or sustainable products).
 Example: A fitness center that offers affordable memberships to low-income 
communities could generate positive publicity and long-term success, even if it 
sacrifices some initial profits.
TIP
The teacher to provide learners with guiding points on other debate motions.
Expected standard 
Entrepreneurship roles in sports analysed correctly.
Sub-Topic: 1.12.1- Sports Industry:
Introduction:
The sports industry is made up of many different parts that work together to make sports excit­
ing and important. These include professional sports teams, sports media, sports equipment and 
sports nutrition. Each part plays a big role in helping athletes perform well and giving learners 
something to enjoy. 
Professional sports teams compete at the highest level, while sports media helps share these 
events with the world. Sports equipment helps athletes perform at their best, and sports nutrition 
helps them stay healthy and strong. All these sectors are connected and help make the sports 
world what it is today.
Specific Competence: Exhibit knowledge of sectors in sports industry.
Learning environment:
The lesson on sectors in sports industry can be conducted from any of the environment such as 
Natural (classroom…) and Technology (video clips, show…)
Teaching and learning materials
Charts. Flash card, handouts, video clips…

2.	 Short-answer questions

3.	 Essay questions

and 
contribution 
(equal 
participation,

and 
communication.)

SUMMARY
 
 Entrepreneurship within the sports industry plays an important role in fostering 
innovation and economic growth
 
 Entrepreneurs in this sector influence their creativity and business acumen to 
introduce new products, services, and experiences that enhance the sports network.
 
 From developing cutting-edge sports equipment and technology to founding 
fitness centers and sports academies, they address the different needs of athletes, 
fans, and other stakeholders.
 
 Sports entrepreneurs also capitalise on marketing, media, and event management 
opportunities, creating compelling content and organising major events that drive 
fan engagement and revenue.
 
 Various sectors within the sports industry offer abundant entrepreneurial 
opportunities. These include sports equipment manufacturing, sports medicine, 
fitness and wellness, sports media and broadcasting, e-sports, and sports tourism.

using available teaching/learning materials such as video clips, charts …. The methods used in 
the lesson were group discussion, question and answer, individual work which stimulated active 
participation. However, some learners needed support on identifying different health-related fit­
ness hence homework was given.
